questions.question: In poker, a player's 'range' refers to:
options: The single hand you are sure they hold
Only the community cards on the table
The full set of hands consistent with their actions so far
The size of their remaining stack

explanations.A: Putting someone on one exact hand ignores all the other holdings their line allows; that is the opposite of range thinking.
explanations.B: The board is shared information, not a description of one player's possible hands.
explanations.C: Correct. A range is the entire collection of combinations a player could have given the actions they have taken.
explanations.D: Stack size affects strategy but is not what 'range' means.

questions.question: Why do strong players think in ranges rather than guessing one hand?
options: Because a single-hand guess is usually wrong and ignores all other possibilities
Because the dealer reveals hole cards on request
Because pot odds stop applying once you assign a range

explanations.A: Correct. Reasoning over a distribution of hands is far more accurate than betting everything on one specific holding.
explanations.B: Dealers never reveal hidden cards; you must reason from actions.
explanations.C: Pot odds always apply; range thinking complements them.
explanations.D: Blinds still set the stakes and influence ranges.

questions.question: As a hand progresses through betting streets, an opponent's range typically:
options: Widens to include every possible two cards
Stays exactly the same on every street
Narrows, because each aggressive action removes inconsistent hands

explanations.A: The range still contains hands; it just becomes smaller and better defined.
explanations.B: More information narrows rather than widens a range.
explanations.C: New actions constantly update the range.
explanations.D: Correct. Bets, raises, and calls each eliminate hands that would not take that line, shrinking the range.

questions.question: A tight player open-raises from under the gun. Compared with a button open, their range is best described as:
options: Made up only of suited connectors

explanations.A: Early-position opens are tighter, not wider.
explanations.B: Correct. UTG must act before many players, so a sound opening range is condensed to stronger hands.
explanations.C: Button ranges are far wider because of position.
explanations.D: Suited connectors are only a small slice of an UTG range.

questions.question: Which statement best reflects range-based thinking on the river?
options: "He has the ace of spades, I just know it."
"His line is consistent with sets, two pair, and a few busted draws as bluffs."
"Whatever I have, I should just call."

explanations.A: Certainty about one exact card is rarely justified and ignores the rest of the range.
explanations.B: Correct. Describing the line as a set of value hands plus some bluffs is the essence of range thinking.
explanations.C: Ranges matter on every street, including the river.
explanations.D: Decisions should depend on how your hand fares against the whole range, not a reflex.

questions.question: Which action narrows a player's range the most?
options: Being dealt cards before any action

explanations.A: A check in a limped pot is consistent with almost anything, so it barely narrows the range.
explanations.B: Before acting, the player still holds a full random range.
explanations.C: Posting a blind is forced and reveals nothing about the cards.
explanations.D: Correct. A turn check-raise is a strong, rare action that filters the range down to relatively few hands.

questions.question: Two players reach the river. 'Range vs range' analysis means you compare:
options: Your entire range of hands against their entire range of hands
Your exact hand against their exact hand
Only the highest card each of you could hold
The dealer's hand against the house

explanations.A: Correct. Equity and strategy are driven by how full ranges interact, not single hands.
explanations.B: Comparing only the two actual hands is hindsight, not strategy at the decision point.
explanations.C: High cards alone do not capture made hands, draws, or pairs.
explanations.D: The dealer does not hold a competing hand.

questions.question: A passive line such as check-call, check-call, check-call most often signals a range that is:
options: Full of the strongest possible hands
Weighted toward medium-strength hands that want to see a showdown cheaply

explanations.A: Players usually bet or raise their strongest hands at some point, so calling lines lack many monsters.
explanations.B: Pure bluffs usually give up rather than call three streets.
explanations.C: Correct. Repeated calling without raising is typical of showdown-bound medium hands.
explanations.D: The line gives plenty of information; the range leans toward medium strength.

questions.question: When you 'assign a range' to an opponent, you should base it primarily on:
options: How long they have been sitting
Their position, the actions they have taken, and bet sizes

explanations.A: Time at the table does not constrain which cards they hold.
explanations.B: Chip color is cosmetic and irrelevant to holdings.
explanations.C: Correct. Position, line, and sizing are the observable inputs that define a realistic range.
explanations.D: Your cards block some combos but do not by themselves define their range.

questions.question: An opponent 4-bets preflop. Against a typical player, this range is usually:
options: Quite narrow: premium pairs and big aces, sometimes a few bluffs

explanations.A: Correct. A standard 4-bet range is condensed to premiums like QQ+/AK plus a small bluff portion.
explanations.B: 4-betting any two cards would be wildly unbalanced and unprofitable.
explanations.C: Small pairs prefer to call or fold, not 4-bet for value.
explanations.D: Suited one-gappers are too weak to make up a 4-bet range.

questions.question: Which is the best reason a single-hand read ('he has AK') is dangerous?
options: It guarantees you fold the best hand
It ignores every other hand the line allows and leads to large mistakes when wrong

explanations.A: AK is rarely the nuts and is often beaten.
explanations.B: A single-hand read does not automatically make you fold the best hand, but it does distort decisions.
explanations.C: Reads can be useful as part of, not a replacement for, range thinking.
explanations.D: Correct. Locking onto one hand discards the rest of the distribution and produces big errors when the read misses.

questions.question: Preflop, before anyone acts, the big blind's range is:

explanations.A: The blind is posted blind, so it is not yet filtered to premiums.
explanations.B: Correct. With no action taken, the big blind can hold any two cards — the widest possible range.
explanations.C: There is no reason the random posted hand would be only suited.
explanations.D: A live player always has some range of hands.

questions.question: Ranges are most useful because poker is a game of:
options: Incomplete information where you reason about probabilities of hidden hands

explanations.A: If information were perfect there would be no need to estimate ranges.
explanations.B: Correct. Hidden cards force you to reason over the probabilities of many possible holdings.
explanations.C: Skillful decisions, not pure luck, drive long-term results.
explanations.D: Outcomes vary, which is exactly why distributions matter.

questions.question: On a final-street decision, you should weight the hands in a range by:
options: The hand you saw last time
Which hand you would least like to face
How many combinations of each hand are possible after removal

explanations.A: A previous hand does not determine current combinations.
explanations.B: Alphabetical order has no bearing on probabilities.
explanations.C: Fear is not a weighting; combo counts are.
explanations.D: Correct. Combinatorics — how many combos remain of each holding — sets the weight of each hand in the range.

questions.question: A 'condensed' or 'capped' description of a range refers to a range that:
options: Contains hands clustered in a strength band, lacking the very top holdings

explanations.A: Correct. A condensed/capped range is bunched in medium strength and missing the strongest possible hands.
explanations.B: By definition a capped range lacks the nut hands.
explanations.C: Combo count is unrelated to whether a range is condensed.
explanations.D: Capped ranges are in fact easier to pressure, not impossible to face.

questions.question: You raise preflop and only the big blind calls. On the flop, whose range is generally narrower and stronger?

explanations.A: The caller's range is wider and more defensive, including many speculative hands.
explanations.B: Raiser and caller ranges differ meaningfully.
explanations.C: Correct. The preflop raiser took the aggressive, range-narrowing action, so their range is tighter and stronger on average.
explanations.D: Both players carry ranges into and through the flop.

questions.question: Which best completes the idea: 'Range thinking replaces I put him on ace-king with ___'?
options: 'he must have exactly the ace of clubs'
'his line is consistent with AK, AQ, some pairs, and a few bluffs'

explanations.A: Ranges apply to every spot.
explanations.B: Naming an exact suit is even narrower than the mistake being replaced.
explanations.C: Correct. Replacing one hand with a weighted set of plausible hands is the core upgrade range thinking provides.
explanations.D: Folding reflexively is not what range thinking prescribes.

questions.question: A player limp-calls preflop, then check-calls all three streets on a dry board. The most consistent description is:
options: A weak-to-medium, capped range that avoided raising at any point
A strong, uncapped range full of sets

explanations.A: Correct. Never raising across the hand caps the range at medium strength.
explanations.B: Sets and other monsters usually raise somewhere, so they are underrepresented here.
explanations.C: Bluffs rarely call down three streets out of position.
explanations.D: The line is entirely plausible; it simply signals a capped range.

questions.question: Thinking about your own range (not just your hand) matters because:
options: It changes the rules of the game
Opponents respond to the hands you could have, so your whole strategy must make sense

explanations.A: Range thinking is analysis, not a rule change.
explanations.B: Your specific hand still matters in the moment.
explanations.C: Both players always have ranges.
explanations.D: Correct. Skilled opponents react to your perceived range, so each action must be consistent across your full strategy.

questions.question: Which sequence narrows a range to the fewest hands?
options: Open-raise, c-bet flop, barrel turn, shove river
Check preflop in a limped pot

explanations.A: A single preflop call leaves a wide range.
explanations.B: Correct. Three streets of aggression culminating in a shove filters the range down to very few, polarized hands.
explanations.C: A check in a limped pot barely narrows anything.
explanations.D: Posting a blind is forced and uninformative.

questions.question: How many combinations of a specific pocket pair (for example, pocket aces) exist before any cards are removed?

explanations.A: 4 is the number of cards of a rank, not the number of two-card pair combinations.
explanations.B: Correct. Choosing 2 of the 4 aces gives C(4,2) = 6 combinations.
explanations.C: 12 is the number of offsuit combinations of an unpaired hand.
explanations.D: 16 is the total combinations of an unpaired hand, not a pair.

questions.question: How many total combinations does a specific unpaired hand such as A-K have before any cards are removed?

explanations.A: 6 is the count for a pocket pair.
explanations.B: 12 counts only the offsuit combinations of A-K.
explanations.C: 4 counts only the suited combinations of A-K.
explanations.D: Correct. Any unpaired hand has 4 × 4 = 16 combinations (4 suited + 12 offsuit).

questions.question: How many suited combinations does a specific suited hand like A-K suited have?

explanations.A: Correct. There is exactly one suited combination per suit, so 4 in total.
explanations.B: 6 is the pocket-pair count.
explanations.C: 12 is the offsuit count for that hand.
explanations.D: 16 is the total of suited plus offsuit.

questions.question: How many offsuit combinations does a specific offsuit hand like A-K offsuit have?

explanations.A: 4 is the suited count, not offsuit.
explanations.B: 6 is the pocket-pair count.
explanations.C: Correct. Offsuit combinations number 4 × 4 − 4 = 12.
explanations.D: 16 is the combined suited-plus-offsuit total.

questions.question: There is one ace on the board. How many combinations of pocket aces can a player still hold?

explanations.A: 6 assumes all four aces are available, but one is on the board.
explanations.B: 1 would be correct only if two aces were on the board.
explanations.C: Correct. With three aces left, C(3,2) = 3 combinations of pocket aces remain.
explanations.D: Players can still make pocket aces from the remaining three aces.

questions.question: The board is A-A-7. How many combinations of pocket aces (quads) are possible?

explanations.A: Correct. With two aces on the board, only the two remaining aces form one pocket-aces combination.
explanations.B: There is no way to make 2 combinations from the two remaining aces other than the single pair.
explanations.C: 3 applies when only one ace is on the board.
explanations.D: 6 assumes no aces are removed.

questions.question: On a K-7-2 board, how many combinations of pocket sevens (a set) can a player have, assuming no other sevens are removed?

explanations.A: 6 would require all four sevens available, but one seven is on the board.
explanations.B: 4 is not the result of any standard combo calculation here.
explanations.C: 1 would require two sevens on the board.
explanations.D: Correct. One seven on the board leaves three, and C(3,2) = 3 set combinations.

questions.question: A range consists of exactly {AA, KK, AK}. How many total combinations is that?

explanations.A: 18 would be three pocket pairs (3 × 6); this range has only two pairs plus AK.
explanations.B: Correct. AA (6) + KK (6) + AK (16) = 28 combinations.
explanations.C: 22 forgets that AK has 16 combos, not 10.
explanations.D: 16 counts only the AK portion.

questions.question: How many combinations make up the range 'QQ+' (that is, QQ, KK, AA)?

explanations.A: 12 counts only two pairs.
explanations.B: Correct. Three pocket pairs at 6 combos each give 3 × 6 = 18.
explanations.C: 6 is a single pocket pair.
explanations.D: 24 would be four pairs, but QQ+ contains three.

questions.question: How many combinations are in 'TT+' (TT, JJ, QQ, KK, AA)?

explanations.A: 24 counts only four pairs.
explanations.B: 20 does not match 6 combos per pair across five pairs.
explanations.C: 36 would be six pairs.
explanations.D: Correct. Five pocket pairs at 6 combos each give 5 × 6 = 30.

questions.question: Suited connectors 98s, 87s, and 76s together contain how many combinations?

explanations.A: Correct. Each suited hand has 4 combos, so 3 × 4 = 12.
explanations.B: 16 is the total for a single unpaired hand counting offsuit too.
explanations.C: 9 would understate the count; each suited hand has 4 combos.
explanations.D: 3 counts the number of hand types, not combinations.

questions.question: How many total combinations of starting hands are dealt in Texas Hold'em?

explanations.A: 169 is the number of distinct hand types (e.g., AKs as one), not combinations.
explanations.B: 2652 is 52 × 51, which double-counts ordered pairs.
explanations.C: Correct. C(52,2) = 1326 distinct two-card combinations.
explanations.D: 52 is the number of cards, not two-card combinations.

questions.question: How many combinations of any pocket pair (all 13 ranks) are there?

explanations.A: 13 is the number of pair ranks, not combinations.
explanations.B: 52 is the number of cards in the deck.
explanations.C: Correct. 13 ranks × 6 combos each = 78 pocket-pair combinations.
explanations.D: 169 is total distinct starting-hand types.

questions.question: On an A-7-2 rainbow board, a player can have A-K. How many A-K combinations remain (one ace is on the board)?

explanations.A: Correct. With three aces and four kings available, 3 × 4 = 12 combinations of A-K remain.
explanations.B: 16 assumes all four aces are available, but one is on the board.
explanations.C: 9 would require a king to also be removed.
explanations.D: 4 is the suited-only count before any removal.

questions.question: On a board of A-K-5, how many combinations of A-K (two pair) are possible, given one ace and one king are on the board?

explanations.A: 16 ignores both blockers on the board.
explanations.B: 12 accounts for only one removed card.
explanations.C: 6 is the count for a pocket pair, not unpaired two pair.
explanations.D: Correct. Three aces and three kings remain, so 3 × 3 = 9 combinations.

questions.question: A value range on the river is {AA, KK, set of 7s on a 7-high board}, where the 7s have 3 combos. AA and KK are full and unblocked (6 each). Total value combos?

explanations.A: 12 forgets the set of sevens.
explanations.B: Correct. 6 (AA) + 6 (KK) + 3 (777) = 15 value combinations.
explanations.C: 18 would assume the set has a full 6 combos, but a board seven reduces it to 3.
explanations.D: 9 omits one of the pocket pairs.

questions.question: If a player's value range has 12 combos and they want a pot-sized river bet to be balanced (2 value : 1 bluff), how many bluff combos should they include?

explanations.A: 12 bluffs would make a 1:1 ratio, far too bluff-heavy for a pot bet.
explanations.B: Correct. A 2:1 value-to-bluff ratio means bluffs = 12 ÷ 2 = 6.
explanations.C: 24 bluffs inverts the ratio and overbluffs massively.
explanations.D: 3 bluffs gives a 4:1 ratio, which underbluffs for a pot bet.

questions.question: You hold A♠K♠. How does this affect the number of A-A combinations in your opponent's range?
options: It removes all of their aces
It removes one ace, cutting opponent's AA from 6 to 3 combos

explanations.A: Your own cards are blockers and do affect opponent combinations.
explanations.B: You hold only one ace, so three aces remain for them.
explanations.C: Blocking a card can only reduce, never increase, the relevant combos.
explanations.D: Correct. Holding one ace leaves three for the opponent, so C(3,2) = 3 AA combos.

questions.question: Holding K♥Q♥, how many combinations of pocket kings does your opponent have?

explanations.A: Correct. One king in your hand leaves three, so C(3,2) = 3 KK combos remain.
explanations.B: 6 assumes you hold no king, but you have the K♥.
explanations.C: 1 would require two kings removed.
explanations.D: Three kings still remain available to the opponent.

questions.question: On a Q-Q-4 board, how many combinations of pocket fours (a full house) are possible, assuming no fours are otherwise removed?

explanations.A: 6 assumes all four fours are available, but one is on the board.
explanations.B: 1 would require two fours on the board.
explanations.C: Correct. One four on the board leaves three, and C(3,2) = 3 combinations.
explanations.D: 4 is not produced by the combination formula here.

questions.question: A flush draw needs two cards of a suit. On a two-tone flop with two hearts, how many remaining hearts are available to make heart combinations?

explanations.A: 13 is the number of hearts in a full deck before any are seen.
explanations.B: 9 is a common 'outs' figure for a flush draw, not the number of hearts left.
explanations.C: Correct. With two hearts on the board, 13 − 2 = 11 hearts remain.
explanations.D: 2 is the number of hearts on the board, not those remaining.

questions.question: With two hearts on the board, how many two-heart combinations (any flush) could a player hold among the remaining 11 hearts?

explanations.A: Correct. C(11,2) = 55 two-heart combinations remain.
explanations.B: 22 is 11 × 2 and double counts unordered pairs incorrectly.
explanations.C: 121 is 11 × 11, counting ordered pairs and same-card pairs.
explanations.D: 11 counts single hearts, not two-card combinations.

questions.question: Which hand type contributes the most combinations to a wide preflop calling range?

explanations.A: Pocket pairs contribute only 6 combos each.
explanations.B: Suited hands add just 4 combos apiece.
explanations.C: A single suited connector is only 4 combos.
explanations.D: Correct. Offsuit unpaired hands carry 12 combos each, the largest per-hand contribution among these.

questions.question: A range is {AKs, AQs, AJs} (suited only). How many combinations is that?

explanations.A: 48 would treat each as a full 16-combo unpaired hand.
explanations.B: Correct. Three suited hands at 4 combos each = 12 combinations.
explanations.C: 16 is the total of a single unpaired hand including offsuit.
explanations.D: 9 does not match 4 combos per suited hand.

questions.question: On a paired board like J-J-3, how many combinations of pocket threes (a full house) can a player hold, with no other threes removed?

explanations.A: 1 would require two threes on the board, but only the jacks are paired.
explanations.B: Correct. One three on the board leaves three of them, so C(3,2) = 3 combos.
explanations.C: 6 assumes all four threes are available.
explanations.D: 4 is not the result of the combination calculation.

questions.question: Why does removal (blockers) matter for range analysis?
options: It adds combinations to the opponent's range
It is irrelevant because all hands are equally likely
It changes the relative weighting of hands by reducing certain combinations

explanations.A: Blockers can only subtract combinations, not add them.
explanations.B: Hands are not equally likely once cards are removed.
explanations.C: Removal affects every street, including the river.
explanations.D: Correct. Cards you and the board hold remove combos, shifting how likely each remaining hand is.

questions.question: A player's value combos on the river total 9, and they have 9 bluff combos. For a pot-sized bet, this range is:
options: Overbluffed — a pot bet wants about 2:1 value-to-bluff, not 1:1

explanations.A: Correct. A pot bet should be roughly 2 value : 1 bluff; 9:9 is too many bluffs.
explanations.B: A 1:1 ratio is not balanced for a pot-sized bet.
explanations.C: 9:9 has too many bluffs, not too few.
explanations.D: The ratio is straightforward to compute from the combo counts.

questions.question: How many combinations of A-K offsuit remain if the board shows the A♣ and K♦?

explanations.A: 12 is the full offsuit count with no blockers.
explanations.B: 9 is the total A-K combos (suited + offsuit) after both blockers, not offsuit alone.
explanations.C: Correct. Three aces and three kings remain; total combos 9, of which 3 are suited, leaving 9 − 3 = 6 offsuit.
explanations.D: 3 is the number of suited A-K combos remaining, not offsuit.

questions.question: Comparing AA and AK preflop, which has more combinations and by how much?

explanations.A: AA has fewer, not more, combinations.
explanations.B: They are not equal: 16 versus 6.
explanations.C: Correct. AK has 16 combos and AA has 6, so AK leads by 10.
explanations.D: The difference is 10, not 4.

questions.question: A range contains all four 'broadway' suited aces {AKs, AQs, AJs, ATs}. How many combinations?

explanations.A: Correct. Four suited hands at 4 combos each = 16 combinations.
explanations.B: 12 would be three suited hands.
explanations.C: 8 would be two suited hands.
explanations.D: 4 is just one suited hand.

questions.question: On a K-Q-J board, how many combinations of A-T (the nut straight) are possible, with no ace or ten removed?

explanations.A: 12 counts only offsuit A-T.
explanations.B: 4 is only the suited count.
explanations.C: 9 would require one ace and one ten removed.
explanations.D: Correct. With four aces and four tens available, A-T has 4 × 4 = 16 combinations.

questions.question: If you block the nut straight by holding one of the needed cards, the opponent's nut-straight combinations:

explanations.A: Blocking a card cannot increase the count.
explanations.B: Correct. Holding a card the nuts needs removes some of those combinations from the opponent.
explanations.C: Removing a key card changes the count.
explanations.D: It typically reduces but rarely eliminates all combos unless you block both needed ranks heavily.

questions.question: A value range is {set of kings (3 combos), set of queens (3 combos)} and you want a half-pot river bet balanced at 3 value : 1 bluff. How many bluff combos?

explanations.A: 6 bluffs would more than match the 6 value combos, far too many.
explanations.B: Correct. 6 value combos at 3:1 means bluffs = 6 ÷ 3 = 2.
explanations.C: 3 bluffs gives 2:1, appropriate for a pot bet, not a half-pot.
explanations.D: 1 bluff gives 6:1, underbluffing the half-pot bet.

questions.question: Which is the correct ordering of preflop combinations from most to fewest?

explanations.A: This wrongly ranks a pair (6) above offsuit (12).
explanations.B: A pair has 6 combos, not 16; 16 is total unpaired.
explanations.C: Suited (4) is the fewest, not the most.
explanations.D: Correct. Offsuit has 12, a pair has 6, and suited has 4 combinations.

questions.question: A player's range on the turn is {AK (16), KQ (16), QJs (4)}. How many combinations total?

explanations.A: Correct. 16 + 16 + 4 = 36 combinations.
explanations.B: 32 forgets the QJs portion.
explanations.C: 40 would treat QJs as a full 8-combo group, which is too many.
explanations.D: 20 undercounts the two 16-combo unpaired hands.

questions.question: A polarized betting range is composed mostly of:
options: Very strong hands and bluffs, with few medium hands

explanations.A: Medium hands are exactly what a polarized range lacks.
explanations.B: Polarized ranges are structured, not random.
explanations.C: Correct. Polarized means two poles: strong value and weak bluffs, with the middle removed.
explanations.D: Folded hands are not part of any active betting range.

questions.question: A merged betting range includes:
options: Strong, medium, and some weaker value hands together

explanations.A: Nuts-only is too narrow to be merged.
explanations.B: Bluffs-only is closer to the air pole of a polarized range.
explanations.C: Correct. A merged range bets a continuous band of strengths, typically at smaller sizes.
explanations.D: Any betting range contains hands.

questions.question: Large overbets on the river most often represent a:
options: Polarized range of nutted hands and bluffs

explanations.A: Correct. Big sizings polarize: you bet your strongest hands and your bluffs, not your middle.
explanations.B: Medium hands rarely overbet; they prefer smaller sizings or checks.
explanations.C: Overbets contain plenty of value, not none.
explanations.D: Second pair is a medium hand poorly suited to overbetting.

questions.question: Small bet sizings (for example, a third of the pot) tend to be associated with:
options: Merged ranges that include many medium-strength hands

explanations.A: Polarization is more associated with large sizings.
explanations.B: A small bet range is not all bluffs; it has lots of value too.
explanations.C: Small bets are not restricted to the nuts.
explanations.D: Correct. Small bets let a wide, merged range bet many medium hands cheaply for thin value and protection.

questions.question: A 'linear' (or merged-for-value) range is best described as:
options: The strongest hands down through good hands, in order of strength, with no bluffs
Two poles with nothing in the middle

explanations.A: A bluff-only range is not linear.
explanations.B: Correct. A linear range takes the top of your range continuously downward — value-weighted with few or no bluffs.
explanations.C: Two poles with a gap describes a polarized range, not a linear one.
explanations.D: A range with hands is not empty.

questions.question: When are you most likely to use a linear (value-heavy, depolarized) 3-bet range?
options: Against a loose player who calls and 4-bet bluffs often
Against a very tight player who only continues with strong hands
Only when you have a bluff

explanations.A: Against a loose, aggressive player a more polarized 3-bet (value + bluffs) is often better.
explanations.B: Correct. Versus a tight opponent who folds weak hands and only continues strong, you want value hands that dominate their calling range, not bluffs.
explanations.C: Linear 3-betting is a standard, useful tool.
explanations.D: Linear ranges are chosen precisely when you want value, not bluffs.

questions.question: Why do medium-strength hands prefer checking or small bets rather than large polarizing bets?
options: Large bets are illegal with medium hands
They want to keep the pot small and realize equity, since they beat bluffs but lose to value

explanations.A: There is no rule against it; it is simply usually unprofitable.
explanations.B: Medium hands are by definition not the nuts.
explanations.C: Medium hands have real showdown value, which is why they avoid bloating the pot.
explanations.D: Correct. Medium hands do best in small pots: they get called by worse and avoid bloating the pot against better hands.

questions.question: A 'depolarized' or 'condensed' range tends to:
options: Concentrate around medium strength, lacking both the very top and pure air
Contain only the nuts and air

explanations.A: Correct. Condensing removes the extremes, clustering the range in the middle strength band.
explanations.B: Nuts-and-air is the definition of a polarized range, the opposite of condensed.
explanations.C: Condensed ranges are structured, not random.
explanations.D: Condensed ranges still contain decent value, just not the top of the range.

questions.question: Against a polarized overbet, calling with a medium-strength hand is usually:
options: Tricky, because you mainly beat the bluffs and lose to the value

explanations.A: A medium hand does not beat the value portion of a polarized range.
explanations.B: Calling is not always correct; it depends on the bettor's bluff ratio.
explanations.C: Correct. Against nuts-or-air, a medium hand is a bluff-catcher: it beats bluffs and loses to value, so it depends on the bluff frequency.
explanations.D: Calling is possible; whether it is profitable depends on the ratio.

questions.question: A merged range is harder to play against than a clearly polarized one because:
options: You face value and medium hands at once, so you cannot assume nuts-or-air

explanations.A: Pot odds always apply.
explanations.B: No range reveals its cards before showdown.
explanations.C: Correct. The continuous spread of strengths makes it hard to find a clean response that a polarized read would give.
explanations.D: Hand rankings never change.

questions.question: On a dynamic, draw-heavy turn, a polarized range that bets big is typically made of:
options: Strong made hands plus draws used as semi-bluffs

explanations.A: Correct. On wet turns the bluff pole is often strong draws (semi-bluffs) alongside the value hands.
explanations.B: Middle pairs are the medium hands a polarized range avoids betting big.
explanations.C: Weak ace-high without equity is a poor candidate for a big polarizing bet.
explanations.D: Folded hands are not part of a betting range.

questions.question: A linear value range bets, in order, from:
options: Best hand downward through strong and decent hands

explanations.A: Linear ranges are ordered by strength, not random.
explanations.B: Starting from the worst hand describes bluffing, not a linear value range.
explanations.C: Betting from the middle outward is closer to a merged-for-protection idea, not linear.
explanations.D: Correct. Linear means a continuous block from the top of the range downward, value-focused.

questions.question: Which best explains why polarized ranges use larger sizings?
options: Larger bets are required by the rules
Larger bets maximize value from strong hands and apply maximum pressure with bluffs
Larger bets reduce your own risk to zero

explanations.A: No rule mandates bet size.
explanations.B: Correct. With nuts-or-air, you want to extract the most with value and put maximum fold pressure on with bluffs.
explanations.C: Bluffs in a polarized range still carry risk of being called.
explanations.D: Opponents defend correctly, so folds are not guaranteed.

questions.question: A merged c-bet on a dry flop (small sizing, high frequency) primarily aims to:
options: Deny equity and get thin value from a wide range cheaply

explanations.A: The range includes value, not only bluffs.
explanations.B: Correct. A small, frequent c-bet bets a merged range to deny equity and extract thin value while keeping the pot manageable.
explanations.C: A merged c-bet uses many hands, not only the nuts.
explanations.D: You bet to profit with value, not to fold it.

questions.question: Which river holding is the clearest member of a polarized betting range's 'bluff pole'?
options: A busted flush draw with no showdown value

explanations.A: Second pair has showdown value and prefers to check or bet small, not bluff big.
explanations.B: A set is value, the strong pole, not a bluff.
explanations.C: Top pair is a value or medium hand, not a bluff.
explanations.D: Correct. A busted draw that cannot win at showdown is an ideal bluff candidate for the air pole.

questions.question: Turning a medium-strength hand into a bluff (betting big with it) is usually a mistake because:
options: You fold out worse hands you beat and only get called by better
Medium hands always win at showdown

explanations.A: Correct. Big bets with medium hands chase away the worse hands you dominate and keep in only the hands that beat you.
explanations.B: It is legal, just typically unprofitable.
explanations.C: Medium hands do not always win; they need to reach a cheap showdown.
explanations.D: Bluffing with the right (low-showdown-value) hands is correct; medium hands are just poor bluff candidates.

questions.question: When the board is static (unlikely to change) and you hold a merged range, smaller bets are attractive because:
options: You must protect against many draws
You can value-bet thinly and get called by worse without needing protection

explanations.A: The nuts can still exist on a static board.
explanations.B: Static boards have few draws, so protection is less of a concern.
explanations.C: Correct. On static boards there is little to protect against, so small thin value bets from a merged range work well.
explanations.D: Bluffs are allowed; the point is that thin value is the main goal here.

questions.question: A range is 'maximally polarized' on the river when it consists of:
options: Hands that beat everything and hands that beat nothing

explanations.A: Polarization is about strength distribution, not combo count.
explanations.B: All-medium is the opposite extreme — fully condensed.
explanations.C: Correct. The extreme of polarization is pure nuts and pure air, with nothing in between.
explanations.D: Identical strength describes a condensed range, not a polarized one.

questions.question: A capped, condensed range defending against bets should usually:
options: Call with its better holdings and fold its weakest, rarely raising for value

explanations.A: Correct. Lacking the nuts, a condensed range mostly bluff-catches with its best hands and folds the rest, with few value raises.
explanations.B: Without top-of-range hands, frequent value raises are not justified.
explanations.C: Shoving a capped range turns medium hands into bad bluffs against a stronger range.
explanations.D: Folding everything overfolds and is exploitable.

questions.question: In a polarized range, the ratio of value to bluffs is chosen mainly to:
options: Maximize the number of bluffs always
Make a bluff-catching opponent indifferent between calling and folding

explanations.A: No ratio guarantees you never lose any individual pot.
explanations.B: Over-bluffing makes calling profitable for the opponent.
explanations.C: The dealer has no say in strategy.
explanations.D: Correct. The value-to-bluff ratio is set so the opponent's bluff-catchers break even, removing their edge.

questions.question: Which scenario most calls for a merged (rather than polarized) value bet?
options: A bluff with a busted draw
A thin value bet on the river with a hand like top pair good kicker against a calling station
An overbet shove with the nuts and air

explanations.A: A busted draw is a bluff, characteristic of a polarized line.
explanations.B: Correct. Against a station, you merge by value-betting good-but-not-great hands that get called by worse.
explanations.C: Nuts-and-air overbets are the definition of polarized.
explanations.D: Checking the nuts is a slow-play, not a merged value bet.

questions.question: Compared with a polarized 3-bet range, a linear 3-bet range contains:
options: Fewer or no bluffs and more strong-but-not-premium value hands

explanations.A: Bluff-only is not linear.
explanations.B: Correct. Linear 3-betting adds value hands (like AQ, KQs, mid pairs) and cuts bluffs, aiming to dominate calls.
explanations.C: Nuts-only is far too narrow for a linear range.
explanations.D: Linear ranges are strength-ordered, not random.

questions.question: A common error when facing a polarized overbet is:
options: Calling with too many medium hands that only beat bluffs

explanations.A: Raising the nuts is a strong, correct play, not an error.
explanations.B: Folding the worst hands is appropriate, not an error.
explanations.C: Calling at the correct indifference frequency is the balanced response.
explanations.D: Correct. Players often hero-call too wide against overbets, paying off the value-heavy side.

questions.question: Which best distinguishes a merged range from a polarized range?
options: A merged range includes medium-strength hands a polarized range would not bet
A merged range has no value hands
A polarized range has no bluffs

explanations.A: Correct. The defining difference is the medium-strength band that merged ranges keep and polarized ranges cut.
explanations.B: Merged ranges are largely value-oriented.
explanations.C: Polarized ranges contain bluffs by definition.
explanations.D: They are structurally different distributions.

questions.question: You hold a pure bluff-catcher facing a river bet. Your call is profitable when the bettor's range:
options: Contains more bluffs than the bet price requires you to beat

explanations.A: Only-nuts means you are drawing dead at showdown.
explanations.B: An all-value range means your bluff-catcher always loses.
explanations.C: Correct. A bluff-catcher profits when bluffs are frequent enough that your pot odds are met.
explanations.D: With no bluffs, calling a bluff-catcher loses money.

questions.question: Why are draws natural bluff candidates in a polarized betting range on early streets?
options: They have equity to improve, so betting them combines fold equity with backup outs

explanations.A: Draws have meaningful equity, which is the point.
explanations.B: A draw is not yet made; it is not the nuts.
explanations.C: Correct. A semi-bluff draw can win by making opponents fold now or by hitting later, an ideal bluff.
explanations.D: Draws are frequently bet as semi-bluffs.

questions.question: On the flop, a high-frequency small c-bet from the preflop raiser is best described as:

explanations.A: Correct. Betting most of your range small is a merged strategy that leverages range advantage.
explanations.B: Range betting is the opposite of maximally polarized.
explanations.C: It includes plenty of value, not only bluffs.
explanations.D: By definition you are betting, not only checking.

questions.question: A depolarized turn bet (smaller, with many medium hands) is most appropriate when:
options: You want to fold the best hand
You have only the nuts and air
You have a range and nut advantage and want to bet many hands for thin value and denial

explanations.A: Betting is meant to profit, not fold winners.
explanations.B: Nuts-and-air pairs naturally with polarized, larger sizing.
explanations.C: A capped, weak range is usually checking, not leading depolarized for value.
explanations.D: Correct. With range/nut advantage you can bet a wide, depolarized range small to pressure the field.

questions.question: If a player only ever makes large bets with the nuts and never bluffs, their large bets are:
options: Polarized in structure but exploitably unbalanced — you should fold all bluff-catchers

explanations.A: A value-only betting range is the opposite of balanced.
explanations.B: Correct. Value-only big bets look polarized but lack the bluff pole, so you simply fold everything but the nuts.
explanations.C: It is not merged; it omits the medium band entirely.
explanations.D: It is highly readable: big bet equals nuts.

questions.question: Which hand most naturally belongs in a merged value-betting range but NOT in a maximally polarized one?

explanations.A: The nut straight is a top-pole hand in both range types.
explanations.B: Correct. Top pair good kicker is a medium-strong value hand that merges, but is too weak to be the value pole of a maximally polarized line.
explanations.C: A busted bluff is the air pole of a polarized range.
explanations.D: Quads are nut-level value, included in a polarized value pole.

questions.question: Polarization tends to increase as the hand approaches the river because:
options: More cards are dealt face down
Medium hands lose value relative to going for max value or giving up, and ranges thin out

explanations.A: The pot grows; it does not disappear.
explanations.B: Hand rankings are fixed throughout.
explanations.C: No additional hidden cards are dealt on the river decision.
explanations.D: Correct. By the river, big bets favor nuts-or-air; medium hands prefer showdown, so ranges polarize.

questions.question: A 'value-merge' on the river means:
options: Betting a strong but non-nut hand for value because worse hands still call

explanations.A: Correct. Value-merging extracts from worse calling hands with a strong, non-nut holding.
explanations.B: Bluffing air is the bluff side, not value-merging.
explanations.C: Checking the nuts forgoes value rather than merging it.
explanations.D: Folding the best hand is the opposite of value-merging.

questions.question: Which board most encourages a polarized strategy from the aggressor on the river?
options: A board with no possible strong hands
A board where both ranges are full of identical medium hands
A board where the aggressor holds the strongest possible hands and clear bluffs
A board that cannot be bet

explanations.A: If no strong hands exist, there is no value pole to polarize around.
explanations.B: Symmetric medium ranges favor small, merged bets.
explanations.C: Correct. Holding the nut hands plus natural bluffs is exactly the setup for a polarized, large-sizing strategy.
explanations.D: Any live board can be bet.

questions.question: A key risk of always betting a merged range for small sizes is:
options: You may leave value on the table with your strongest hands

explanations.A: Betting is not folding.
explanations.B: Merged betting is value-leaning, not overbluffed.
explanations.C: Correct. Small merged bets undercharge your nut hands, so a balanced strategy mixes in larger polarized bets with the top.
explanations.D: Merged betting wins plenty; it just may under-extract with the nuts.

questions.question: When defending against a polarized range, your continuing range should be:
options: Weighted toward bluff-catchers and your own strong hands, folding the rest
Identical to facing a merged bet

explanations.A: Correct. Against nuts-or-air you defend with bluff-catchers and value, folding hands that beat nothing in their range.
explanations.B: Defending all medium hands overcalls against the value pole.
explanations.C: Your weakest hands have no equity to continue.
explanations.D: Defense against polarized ranges differs from defense against merged ranges.

questions.question: 'Range advantage' on a given board means:
options: You always hold the single best hand
Your range has more equity on that board than your opponent's range

explanations.A: Blinds are unrelated to range advantage.
explanations.B: Having more equity on average does not mean you always hold the nuts.
explanations.C: Range advantage is evaluated relative to a board that exists.
explanations.D: Correct. Range advantage is an equity comparison across whole ranges on a specific texture.

questions.question: 'Nut advantage' refers to which player:
options: Holds the strongest possible hands (the nuts and near-nuts) more often

explanations.A: Chip counts are stack depth, not nut advantage.
explanations.B: Correct. Nut advantage is about who can have the top of the range more frequently.
explanations.C: Position is valuable but distinct from nut advantage.
explanations.D: Folding frequency is unrelated to holding the nuts.

questions.question: On a K-7-2 rainbow flop in a single-raised pot, who typically has the range advantage?

explanations.A: The caller's range has fewer strong kings and big pairs on this texture.
explanations.B: Correct. The raiser holds more KK, AK, and big pairs that smash a king-high dry board.
explanations.C: Equity is clearly skewed toward the raiser here.
explanations.D: The dealer holds no competing range.

questions.question: On a low connected flop like 6-5-4 with two of a suit, range advantage often shifts toward:
options: The preflop raiser, who has all the big pairs
The big blind defender, whose range includes more suited connectors and small pairs

explanations.A: Big pairs are often overpairs but face many straights and draws here; the texture favors the defender's connected holdings.
explanations.B: Acting order is position, not range advantage.
explanations.C: Chips do not determine board interaction.
explanations.D: Correct. The big blind's wide defending range hits low connected boards (straights, two pair, draws) more than a tight raising range.

questions.question: Having a range advantage on the flop usually supports which strategy?
options: Betting a high frequency, often with smaller sizing

explanations.A: Correct. With more equity across your range, you can bet often (frequently small) to pressure the weaker range.
explanations.B: Checking everything wastes a range advantage.
explanations.C: Folding squanders an equity edge.
explanations.D: Range advantage does not require always overbetting; small frequent bets are common.

questions.question: Even without an overall equity (range) advantage, why can nut advantage justify large bets and overbets?
options: It changes the pot odds rules
It guarantees you win every pot
Holding the nuts more often lets you apply pressure your opponent cannot answer with their capped range

explanations.A: Pot odds are unchanged.
explanations.B: Nut advantage improves leverage, not certainty of winning.
explanations.C: Correct. The player with the nuts can credibly threaten max pressure, and the capped opponent cannot match it.
explanations.D: Variance still exists.

questions.question: On an A-K-Q board, the preflop raiser usually has a range advantage because:
options: Their range is all small pairs
Their range contains more big broadway cards and strong pairs that connect

explanations.A: A-K-Q is a high, not a low, board.
explanations.B: A raising range is not made of only small pairs.
explanations.C: Correct. Broadway-heavy boards favor the tighter raising range full of high cards.
explanations.D: Blinds are irrelevant to range advantage.

questions.question: Why might a player with a range advantage still sometimes check?
options: To protect their checking range and avoid being too predictable, especially with medium hands

explanations.A: Correct. Even an advantaged range checks some hands to keep its checking range robust and trap or pot-control.
explanations.B: Checking is not always best; betting often is with an advantage.
explanations.C: Range advantage encourages betting, it does not forbid it.
explanations.D: Checking does not reveal cards.

questions.question: On a monotone flop (three of one suit) in a single-raised pot, the player MORE likely to have the nut flush in their range is usually:
options: The preflop raiser, who holds more suited aces

explanations.A: The defender does have some suited hands but typically fewer nut-flush combos than a raiser.
explanations.B: The dealer holds no range.
explanations.C: The distribution of suited aces is not equal between ranges.
explanations.D: Correct. Raising ranges are rich in suited aces (especially Ax of the suit), giving nut advantage on monotone boards.

questions.question: Range advantage and nut advantage can diverge. Which statement captures this?
options: They are always the same thing
You can have more total equity (range advantage) while your opponent holds the nuts more often (nut advantage)
Nut advantage implies you must fold
Range advantage means you hold the single best hand

explanations.A: They frequently differ depending on board texture.
explanations.B: Correct. The two concepts are distinct; one player may win on average while the other monopolizes the very top hands.
explanations.C: Nut advantage is a reason to apply pressure, not to fold.
explanations.D: Range advantage is an average-equity edge, not a guarantee of the best single hand.

questions.question: After you c-bet the flop and get called, then the turn brings a card that helps the caller's range more than yours, your range advantage:
options: Can shrink or flip to the caller
Is fixed for the whole hand
Does not depend on the turn card

explanations.A: Advantage does not automatically grow each street.
explanations.B: Correct. Turn and river cards continually shift equity; a card favoring the caller can erase your advantage.
explanations.C: Range advantage is dynamic, recalculated each street.
explanations.D: The turn card directly changes how the ranges connect.

questions.question: Which is the best practical use of having the nut advantage on the river?
options: Use larger bet sizes and overbets to maximize pressure and value

explanations.A: You would never fold the strong hands that create the advantage.
explanations.B: Checking wastes nut advantage.
explanations.C: Small bets under-leverage the threat of the nuts.
explanations.D: Correct. With the top hands concentrated in your range, big sizings extract value and punish the capped opponent.

questions.question: Which board most clearly gives the big blind defender a range advantage over a UTG raiser?

explanations.A: Correct. Low connected boards connect with the defender's suited connectors and small pairs far more than a tight UTG range.
explanations.B: Paired king-high boards favor the raiser's strong kings.
explanations.C: Broadway boards favor the high-card-heavy UTG range.
explanations.D: Ace-king boards strongly favor the premium-laden raiser.

questions.question: Range advantage is most useful as a guide to:
options: How many chips to buy in for
Which single card the opponent holds
Your overall betting frequency and sizing on a given board

explanations.A: Buy-in size is unrelated.
explanations.B: It is about distributions, not pinpointing one card.
explanations.C: Correct. Range advantage informs how often and how large you should bet across your range.
explanations.D: Betting order is positional, not a function of range advantage.

questions.question: A capped player facing an opponent with a clear nut advantage should generally:
options: Play more cautiously, bluff-catch selectively, and avoid bloating the pot

explanations.A: Stacking off light into a nut-advantaged range loses money.
explanations.B: Frequent value raises are unjustified without top-of-range hands.
explanations.C: Correct. Without the nuts, the capped player should control the pot and pick spots to bluff-catch.
explanations.D: Overbetting medium hands turns them into poor bluffs against a stronger range.

questions.question: Why does the preflop raiser usually start the flop with at least a small range advantage in a single-raised pot?
options: They chose to raise a stronger range, while the caller continued with a wider, weaker one

explanations.A: Correct. The aggressive preflop action condensed their range to stronger hands than the caller's defend range.
explanations.B: Position is separate; the raiser is sometimes out of position.
explanations.C: Some boards flip the advantage to the caller.
explanations.D: Stack sizes do not create range advantage.

questions.question: On a paired flop like 8-8-3, the preflop raiser's range advantage mostly comes from:
options: The board being a flush board
Higher overpairs and more big cards that still dominate the board

explanations.A: Blinds are unrelated.
explanations.B: Both players have similarly few trip-eight combos; that is not the source of the edge.
explanations.C: 8-8-3 is not described as a flush board.
explanations.D: Correct. The raiser's overpairs and high cards dominate this low paired board even though trips are rare for both.

questions.question: Nut advantage matters most for which decision?
options: Whether you can profitably use very large bets and overbets

explanations.A: Shuffling is procedural and unrelated.
explanations.B: Correct. The credibility of big bets and overbets depends on who can have the nuts.
explanations.C: Rake is fixed by the room, not by nut advantage.
explanations.D: Blind levels are a tournament structure detail.

questions.question: Two players check the flop. On the turn, the player with the range advantage who now bets is leveraging:
options: Their stronger overall range to apply pressure the weaker range struggles to meet
A rule that forces a fold

explanations.A: Range advantage is statistical, not a guarantee of holding the best hand.
explanations.B: Correct. The bet uses the equity edge of the stronger range to pressure the weaker one.
explanations.C: No rule forces opponents to fold.
explanations.D: The dealer plays no part in strategy.

questions.question: Which factor does NOT directly contribute to range advantage on a board?
options: How well each player's range connects with the board
The number of strong combos each range holds
The color of the table felt

explanations.A: How ranges connect to the board is central to range advantage.
explanations.B: Strong-combo counts directly affect range advantage.
explanations.C: Average equity across ranges is the definition of range advantage.
explanations.D: Correct. The felt color is irrelevant; it has no bearing on equities.

questions.question: A flop comes Q-J-T with two of a suit. In a button-versus-big-blind single-raised pot, range advantage is:
options: Close, but nut advantage (KK-A straights, sets, flush draws) can be contested by both ranges
Entirely the button's with no contest
Entirely the big blind's with no contest

explanations.A: Correct. Both wide ranges connect heavily with this coordinated board, so equities and nut combos are split and contested.
explanations.B: The big blind's wide range also smashes this connected board.
explanations.C: The button's range likewise contains plenty of strong broadway combos.
explanations.D: Both ranges clearly have meaningful equity here.

questions.question: On the river, you hold the nut advantage and bet an overbet. A balanced opponent should:
options: Continue with their best bluff-catchers at the correct frequency and fold the rest

explanations.A: Raising the whole range turns made hands into bad bluffs against your nuts.
explanations.B: Calling everything overpays the value-heavy overbet.
explanations.C: Correct. Against an overbet from a nut-advantaged range, defend the strongest bluff-catchers per the price and fold weak hands.
explanations.D: Folding everything overfolds and is exploitable.

questions.question: Equity realization can reduce the practical value of a raw range advantage when:
options: You are in position with the betting lead
You are out of position and cannot easily realize your equity

explanations.A: Static boards do not by themselves prevent equity realization.
explanations.B: Position helps you realize equity, enhancing the advantage.
explanations.C: Correct. Out of position, a range may fail to realize its equity, dampening the benefit of a nominal edge.
explanations.D: Holding the nuts realizes equity fully.

questions.question: Which describes a 'nut-advantage but not range-advantage' spot?
options: Your opponent has slightly more total equity, but only you can hold the absolute nuts
You have both more equity and the nuts

explanations.A: Correct. This is the classic split: opponent edges average equity while you monopolize the top hands.
explanations.B: That is holding both advantages, not just nut advantage.
explanations.C: Having neither describes the disadvantaged player.
explanations.D: If the opponent has both, you hold neither advantage.

questions.question: Why is nut advantage often described as the key to 'who gets to apply maximum pressure'?
options: Because it doubles the pot automatically
Because the nuts always win the pot uncontested
Only the player who can credibly hold the nuts can threaten the biggest bets without being raised off them

explanations.A: Pot size grows through betting, not automatically.
explanations.B: The nuts win at showdown but the point is leverage in betting, and opponents can still fold.
explanations.C: Applying pressure with bets is always legal.
explanations.D: Correct. The threat of the nuts backs up large bets; a capped opponent cannot credibly counter-pressure.

questions.question: A useful first question to assess range advantage on a flop is:
options: 'Which range contains more of the hands that love this board?'

explanations.A: Card color does not affect equities.
explanations.B: Correct. Asking which range is more enriched in board-loving hands is the practical heuristic for range advantage.
explanations.C: Physical traits are irrelevant.
explanations.D: The time has no bearing on range interaction.

questions.question: On A-7-2 rainbow, why does the preflop raiser typically c-bet a large fraction of their range?
options: Because they are forced to bet
They have a clear range and nut advantage, so betting pressures the capped defender
Because the board is wet and scary

explanations.A: No rule forces a c-bet.
explanations.B: Correct. Dry ace-high boards favor the raiser, enabling frequent, often small, c-bets against a weaker range.
explanations.C: They do not always have aces; it is the range edge that matters.
explanations.D: A-7-2 rainbow is dry, not wet.

questions.question: Which player tends to have nut advantage on a 2-2-2 board in a single-raised pot?
options: Neither, it is impossible to assess
The player with more high pocket pairs and big cards (usually the raiser)

explanations.A: It is assessable: the stronger high-card range holds the effective nuts more often.
explanations.B: Quad deuces are vanishingly rare and roughly symmetric; kickers decide.
explanations.C: The big blind's wider, weaker range does not have an automatic edge here.
explanations.D: Correct. On extreme paired boards, the effective nuts becomes the best kicker/overpair, favoring the raiser's stronger range.

questions.question: Multiway pots tend to make range advantage:
options: Less reliable as a c-bet justification, since more ranges can connect with the board

explanations.A: Correct. With more players, someone is more likely to have hit, so a raw range advantage justifies betting less often.
explanations.B: Extra players dilute the raiser's edge, not strengthen it.
explanations.C: Range advantage still matters; it is just shared among more ranges.
explanations.D: The dealer holds no range.

questions.question: Which combination of advantages most strongly supports a high-frequency, large-sizing river betting strategy?
options: Range disadvantage with no nut advantage
Both range advantage and nut advantage

explanations.A: A nut disadvantage discourages large betting.
explanations.B: Lacking both edges argues for checking and pot control.
explanations.C: Correct. Holding both more equity and the top hands lets you bet often and large with maximum leverage.
explanations.D: With neither advantage, aggression is poorly supported.

questions.question: A 'capped' range is one that:
options: Has an upper limit on strength and is unlikely to contain the nuts

explanations.A: Combo count is unrelated to being capped.
explanations.B: By definition a capped range lacks the nuts.
explanations.C: Correct. Capped means the maximum strength is limited; the very best hands are largely absent.
explanations.D: Capped ranges can still call and continue.

questions.question: An 'uncapped' range is one that:
options: Can still contain the strongest possible hands, including the nuts

explanations.A: Correct. Uncapped means the top of the range — including the nuts — is still possible.
explanations.B: Uncapped ranges bet and raise freely.
explanations.C: Uncapped ranges include strong made hands, not just high cards.
explanations.D: Uncapped refers to strength, not fold status.

questions.question: Why is a 'call, call, call' line on multiple streets typically a capped range?
options: Because calling is illegal with strong hands
Players usually raise their strongest hands at some point, so pure calling rarely hides the nuts

explanations.A: The dealer does not influence range strength.
explanations.B: Calling with strong hands is legal; it is just less common as a pure line.
explanations.C: Callers do not fold the nuts; they simply rarely arrive via pure calling.
explanations.D: Correct. Strong hands tend to raise for value somewhere, so a passive calling line is missing most monsters.

questions.question: Against a clearly capped opponent on the river, a profitable adjustment is to:
options: Bluff and bet for thin value more often, since they rarely have a hand strong enough to punish you

explanations.A: Checking forgoes the exploit a capped range invites.
explanations.B: Correct. With few strong hands in their range, you can apply more pressure with bluffs and thin value.
explanations.C: Folding everything wastes the advantage of facing a weak range.
explanations.D: Betting more, not never, is the exploit.

questions.question: Which line most strongly UNCAPS a player's range?
options: Raising or check-raising aggressively at some point

explanations.A: Check-calling throughout is the classic capped line.
explanations.B: Correct. A raise or check-raise represents the top of the range, signaling the nuts are possible (uncapped).
explanations.C: Folding ends the hand; it does not uncap a range.
explanations.D: Limping is a passive, often capping action.

questions.question: The preflop raiser's range on the flop is generally considered:
options: Uncapped — it can still contain overpairs and the strongest hands

explanations.A: It contains value hands, not only bluffs.
explanations.B: An aggressive preflop range is the opposite of capped.
explanations.C: The range is full of hands.
explanations.D: Correct. By raising preflop they keep the premium hands, so their flop range is uncapped.

questions.question: A big blind who only flat-calls a raise preflop (never 3-bets) often has a flop range that is:
options: Somewhat capped, because the strongest hands would have 3-bet

explanations.A: Correct. If premiums always 3-bet, the flat-call range is missing them and is capped to a degree.
explanations.B: The flatting range typically lacks the very top hands.
explanations.C: It is not nuts-only; it is a wide capped range.
explanations.D: It is estimable: flatting caps the top.

questions.question: Why does an uncapped range allow a player to defend more confidently against aggression?
options: Because they can credibly hold the nuts and raise back, deterring relentless bluffing

explanations.A: Rules do not favor any range.
explanations.B: An uncapped range does not guarantee winning.
explanations.C: Correct. The threat of the nuts in an uncapped range discourages opponents from over-bluffing.
explanations.D: Pot size is independent of being uncapped.

questions.question: A player check-raises the flop, then check-calls turn and river. By the river their range is:
options: Often somewhat capped on the river, having shown willingness to play passively after the raise

explanations.A: The later passivity caps, rather than uncaps, the range.
explanations.B: Passive river calls argue against a nuts-only read.
explanations.C: Correct. The passive turn and river calls suggest the very strongest hands would have kept betting, capping the river range somewhat.
explanations.D: It contains made hands, not only air.

questions.question: The main strategic consequence of facing a capped range is that you should:
options: Increase your bluffing and overbetting frequency to exploit the missing strong hands

explanations.A: Correct. Because they rarely hold the nuts, you can pressure them more with bluffs and big bets.
explanations.B: Less aggression fails to exploit the cap.
explanations.C: Checking forgoes the exploit.
explanations.D: You can value bet more, not less, against a capped range.

questions.question: Which player most clearly has an uncapped range on the river?
options: A player who folded the turn
A player who limp-called and check-called every street
A player who only called preflop and checked down
A player who 3-bet preflop, c-bet flop, barreled turn, and bet river

explanations.A: A folded hand is out; it has no river range.
explanations.B: Limp-call and check-call lines are classic capped lines.
explanations.C: Calling and checking down caps the range.
explanations.D: Correct. Consistent aggression across all streets keeps the top of the range intact — uncapped.

questions.question: When you have a capped range and your opponent is uncapped, you should generally:
options: Avoid building a huge pot and bluff-catch carefully rather than stacking off

explanations.A: Stacking off any pair into an uncapped range loses heavily.
explanations.B: Correct. With the weaker, capped range, control the pot and pick bluff-catches instead of committing your stack.
explanations.C: Overbetting for value with a capped range is rarely justified.
explanations.D: Raising every street misrepresents a range that lacks the nuts.

questions.question: A board runs out to put four cards to a straight, and a player who only called now bets small. Their range is likely:
options: Capped — they would often have raised earlier with the straight
Uncapped and full of nut hands

explanations.A: The passive line argues against them holding the straight.
explanations.B: Correct. Having only called, they rarely have the made straight that would have raised, so they remain capped.
explanations.C: Calling-only lines cap the range, the opposite of uncapped.
explanations.D: The range includes some made hands and thin value, not only bluffs.

questions.question: Maintaining an uncapped range in your own play is valuable because:
options: It makes you fold less by rule
It lets you defend against aggression and discourages opponents from bluffing you relentlessly

explanations.A: Variance remains regardless.
explanations.B: It makes the nuts possible, not guaranteed.
explanations.C: No rule changes your folding; it is strategic protection.
explanations.D: Correct. Keeping strong hands in your checking/calling ranges protects you from being run over.

questions.question: If you slow-play your strongest hands too often, the downside is:
options: Your betting range becomes capped and easier to play against, and you under-extract value

explanations.A: Correct. Constantly trapping caps your aggressive lines and costs value when you fail to bet strong hands.
explanations.B: Excessive slow-playing usually costs value, not gains it.
explanations.C: Slow-playing is legal.
explanations.D: Slow-playing removes strong hands from your betting range, capping (not uncapping) it.

questions.question: The big blind defends by calling and the flop checks through. On the turn, the big blind leads small. This lead is often:
options: A capped, merged range of medium hands rather than the nuts

explanations.A: It usually contains real medium equity, not only air.
explanations.B: It is not always a set; that read is too strong.
explanations.C: Correct. A small turn lead after a checked flop is typically medium-strength and capped, since monsters often check to raise or bet bigger.
explanations.D: The line is interpretable as a capped, merged lead.

questions.question: Which action keeps your river checking range uncapped (protected)?
options: Occasionally checking some very strong hands (slow-plays/traps) along with medium hands

explanations.A: Checking only weak hands is the definition of a capped check.
explanations.B: Always betting strong hands leaves your checking range capped.
explanations.C: Correct. Mixing some strong hands into your checks prevents your checking range from being capped and exploitable.
explanations.D: You would never fold strong hands; that wastes equity.

questions.question: Recognizing that an opponent's range is capped should most change your willingness to:
options: Bluff at and apply pressure to the pot

explanations.A: Correct. A capped opponent is the prime target for increased bluffing and pressure.
explanations.B: Showing cards gives away information for no reason.
explanations.C: Leaving is unrelated to exploiting a capped range.
explanations.D: Seat changes do not exploit a capped range in the current hand.

questions.question: A 'balanced' range is one that:
options: Mixes value hands and bluffs so opponents cannot exploit you by always calling or always folding

explanations.A: You never show cards early.
explanations.B: An all-bluff range is unbalanced and exploitable by calling.
explanations.C: Checking the nuts forgoes value; it is not what balance means.
explanations.D: Correct. Balance pairs value with the right number of bluffs so no single response by the opponent beats you.

questions.question: For a pot-sized river bet, the GTO value-to-bluff ratio is approximately:

explanations.A: 1:1 over-bluffs a pot-sized bet, making calls profitable for the opponent.
explanations.B: Correct. A pot-sized bet should be about two value combos for each bluff, so bluffs are one-third of the betting range.
explanations.C: 3:1 is the ratio for a half-pot bet, not a pot bet.
explanations.D: 1:2 has far too many bluffs.

questions.question: For a half-pot river bet, the balanced value-to-bluff ratio is approximately:

explanations.A: 1:1 over-bluffs at this small size.
explanations.B: Correct. A half-pot bet gives the caller 3:1 pot odds, so you bluff one combo per three value combos.
explanations.C: 2:1 corresponds to a pot-sized bet, not a half-pot.
explanations.D: 4:1 corresponds to roughly a third-pot bet, under-bluffing a half-pot.

questions.question: For a 2x-pot overbet on the river, the balanced value-to-bluff ratio is approximately:

explanations.A: 3:1 corresponds to a half-pot bet, far too few bluffs for an overbet.
explanations.B: 2:1 is the pot-sized ratio, still too few bluffs for a 2x overbet.
explanations.C: 1:1 slightly over-bluffs even a large overbet.
explanations.D: Correct. A 2x-pot overbet lays 1.5:1, so bluffs make up about 40% of the range, roughly 3 value to 2 bluffs.

questions.question: Minimum Defense Frequency (MDF) tells you:
options: The fraction of your range you must continue with to stop opponents from profitably bluffing any two cards

explanations.A: Correct. MDF is the defending share needed so that a pure bluff cannot show an automatic profit.
explanations.B: MDF concerns defending (calling/raising), not your bluffing frequency.
explanations.C: It is a frequency, not a bet size.
explanations.D: Blinds are unrelated to MDF.

questions.question: Against a pot-sized bet, the minimum defense frequency is approximately:

explanations.A: 33% would massively overfold against a pot bet.
explanations.B: 67% is the MDF against a half-pot bet, not a pot bet.
explanations.C: Correct. MDF = pot ÷ (pot + bet) = 1 ÷ 2 = 50% against a pot-sized bet.
explanations.D: 100% would never fold, which is not required.

questions.question: Against a half-pot bet, the minimum defense frequency is approximately:

explanations.A: 50% is the MDF against a pot-sized bet, not a half-pot.
explanations.B: 33% would overfold dramatically.
explanations.C: Correct. MDF = 1 ÷ (1 + 0.5) ≈ 67% against a half-pot bet.
explanations.D: 25% overfolds even more.

questions.question: If you never bluff, a thinking opponent can exploit you by:
options: Folding all bluff-catchers to your big bets, since you only ever have value

explanations.A: Correct. With no bluffs, your big bets always mean value, so opponents correctly fold everything but the nuts.
explanations.B: They cannot profitably call when you always have value.
explanations.C: They do not win automatically; they just avoid paying off.
explanations.D: Hand rankings are fixed.

questions.question: If you bluff far too often, a thinking opponent exploits you by:
options: Calling down lightly with many bluff-catchers

explanations.A: Leaving forgoes the exploit entirely.
explanations.B: Folding everything against an over-bluffer is the opposite mistake.
explanations.C: Bluffing back does not directly punish your over-bluffing; calling does.
explanations.D: Correct. An over-bluffed range is beaten by wide, light calls that scoop your frequent bluffs.

questions.question: As your bet size increases, the share of bluffs in a balanced betting range should:

explanations.A: Bluff share rises, not falls, with size.
explanations.B: Correct. Larger bets risk more, so you can include proportionally more bluffs while keeping the opponent indifferent.
explanations.C: The balanced bluff share is size-dependent, not constant.
explanations.D: Bluffs never drop to zero in a balanced betting range.

questions.question: Balance matters most against:
options: Observant, adjusting opponents who exploit patterns

explanations.A: Folded players take no further action to exploit.
explanations.B: Correct. Balance protects you from thinking players; against non-adjusting opponents, exploitative play is better.
explanations.C: The dealer does not play against you.
explanations.D: Mucked cards are out of play.

questions.question: Against a weak opponent who calls far too much (a calling station), the best adjustment to your range is to:
options: Bet more value hands thinly and cut most of your bluffs

explanations.A: Checking forgoes the value a station hands you.
explanations.B: Bluffing more against a station is a losing adjustment.
explanations.C: You should value bet more, not stop.
explanations.D: Correct. Stations pay off value, so widen your value bets and reduce bluffs that they will not fold to.

questions.question: Against an opponent who folds far too often, the best adjustment is to:
options: Bluff more frequently, since fold equity is high

explanations.A: Correct. Over-folders give up too much, so increasing bluff frequency is directly profitable.
explanations.B: Value-only play leaves bluffing profit on the table against over-folders.
explanations.C: Not betting forgoes easy fold equity.
explanations.D: Slow-playing fails to capitalize on their tendency to fold.

questions.question: A river value range has 8 combos. To balance a pot-sized bet, how many bluff combos are needed?

explanations.A: 8 bluffs gives a 1:1 ratio, over-bluffing a pot bet.
explanations.B: 16 bluffs would heavily over-bluff.
explanations.C: Correct. A 2:1 value-to-bluff ratio means 8 ÷ 2 = 4 bluff combos.
explanations.D: 2 bluffs is a 4:1 ratio, suitable for a half-pot bet, not a pot bet.

questions.question: A river value range has 12 combos. To balance a half-pot bet (3:1 value:bluff), how many bluff combos?

explanations.A: 6 bluffs gives a 2:1 ratio, suited to a pot bet, not a half-pot.
explanations.B: 12 bluffs is a 1:1 ratio, far too many for a half-pot bet.
explanations.C: Correct. 12 ÷ 3 = 4 bluff combos for a 3:1 ratio.
explanations.D: 3 bluffs gives a 4:1 ratio, slightly under-bluffing a half-pot.

questions.question: Against a one-third-pot bet, the minimum defense frequency is approximately:

explanations.A: Correct. MDF = 1 ÷ (1 + 1/3) = 1 ÷ (4/3) = 75% against a third-pot bet.
explanations.B: 67% is the MDF for a half-pot bet.
explanations.C: 50% is the MDF for a pot-sized bet.
explanations.D: 33% would heavily overfold against such a small bet.

questions.question: MDF is most relevant against opponents who:
options: Could be bluffing with a wide range, so you must defend enough to deny auto-profit

explanations.A: Folded players are not betting at you.
explanations.B: Against a player who never bluffs, you should overfold and ignore MDF.
explanations.C: If they always have the nuts, defending is pointless.
explanations.D: Correct. MDF protects you from wide-bluffing opponents by defending enough that pure bluffs do not auto-profit.

questions.question: Why is strict GTO balance sometimes the wrong goal at low stakes?
options: Opponents make large mistakes, so exploitative deviations earn more than balanced play

explanations.A: Balanced play is perfectly legal.
explanations.B: Correct. When opponents are far from optimal, exploiting their leaks outperforms perfectly balanced play.
explanations.C: Balanced play does not lose every pot.
explanations.D: Exploitable low-stakes players are very beatable.

questions.question: When choosing bluff combos to balance a value-heavy range, you should prefer hands that:
options: Have the least showdown value and good blockers to the opponent's calls

explanations.A: High-showdown-value hands should check and try to win unimproved, not bluff.
explanations.B: Correct. Bluff with hands that cannot win at showdown and that block the opponent's continuing range.
explanations.C: The nuts are value, not bluffs.
explanations.D: Bluffs can lose; choosing low-equity hands minimizes the cost of giving up.

questions.question: A range that bets only its nuts and its pure air, in the right proportion for the size, is:

explanations.A: Such ranges are standard and constructable.
explanations.B: It is polarized, not merged, and it can be balanced.
explanations.C: A range containing the nuts is uncapped, not capped.
explanations.D: Correct. Nuts and air at the correct value-to-bluff ratio is a textbook balanced polarized range.

questions.question: If you defend MORE than the MDF against a habitual over-bluffer, you are:
options: Exploiting them correctly by catching extra bluffs

explanations.A: Correct. MDF is a floor against unknown opponents; against a known over-bluffer you should defend even wider to profit.
explanations.B: Defending wider versus an over-bluffer is the right exploit, not a mistake.
explanations.C: There is no rule against over-defending.
explanations.D: You profit, on average, by catching their extra bluffs.

questions.question: Why do smaller bets require a lower proportion of bluffs to stay balanced?
options: Small bets always have the nuts
Smaller bets are illegal to bluff
A small bet risks little and lays the caller good odds, so fewer bluffs keep them indifferent
Bluffs scale with the pot, not the bet

explanations.A: Small bets are not restricted to the nuts.
explanations.B: Bluffing small bets is legal and common.
explanations.C: Correct. Since the caller is getting a good price, you need proportionally fewer bluffs to make them indifferent.
